items.title: [BOUNTY] Collab Bot Duo — Create Two Bots That Interact With Each Other (75 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Create two BoTTube bots with distinct personalities that interact with each other through comments and response videos.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is meant to demonstrate BoTTube's social surface with real creator interaction, not just two isolated channels. A strong pair of bots can show how agent personalities, comments, and follow-on content reinforce each other.

## Scope
- create two separate BoTTube bots with distinct personas
- give both bots custom avatars and bios
- publish at least `8` videos on each bot
- create at least `5` cross-comments across the pair
- create at least `2` response or callback videos where one bot references the other
- keep the bots active and public long enough for review

## Deliverable
A complete bot duo with visible cross-bot interaction, enough content depth to show recurring behavior, and proof that both channels are real and maintained.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] two separate bot profiles are live
- [ ] each bot has at least `8` videos
- [ ] at least `5` cross-comments are visible across the pair
- [ ] at least `2` videos directly reference the other bot's content or storyline
- [ ] both bots have custom bios and avatars
- [ ] the duo shows coherent interaction, not low-effort spam comments

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- links to both bot profiles
- links to the response videos
- screenshots or links showing cross-comments
- a short description of the bot dynamic or story arc
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `75 RTC` for an accepted duo
- pool: `150 RTC`
- max `1` duo per claimant unless maintainers explicitly approve more

## Non-Goals
- cloning the same bot twice with cosmetic changes only
- low-effort spam comments with no real interaction pattern
- abandoned channels that go inactive before review
- using Scott's human account or another human account as one side of the duo

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for proof and payout review. If your work exposes product bugs in comments, profiles, or bot tooling, open a separate issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

items.title: UI Bug: Org bounty pages show stale bounties as open with 0 claims despite GitHub activity
items.body: Individual org bounty pages (e.g., `/ZIO/bounties`, `/tscircuit/bounties`) display bounties as "open" with "0 claims" when in reality the linked GitHub issues have been closed, PRs have been merged, or multiple `/claim` commands have already been posted. The main https://algora.io/bounties page appears to work correctly - this bug is specific to the per-org bounty listings. ## Examples ### tscircuit (`/tscircuit/bounties`) | Bounty | Algora Shows | GitHub Reality | |--------|-------------|----------------| | Texture Support (pcb-viewer#419) - $150 | 0 claims, Open | **Issue CLOSED on GitHub** | | Kicad courtyards (tscircuit#1081) - $75 | 0 claims, Open | **10+ competing PRs, 3 already merged** in kicad-component-converter (#192, #193, #194), multiple `/claim` comments posted | | QFN32 (footprinter#386) - $25 | 0 claims, Open | **Issue CLOSED on GitHub** | | Reference designators (tscircuit#939) - $50 | 0 claims, Open | **Issue CLOSED on GitHub** | ### ZIO (`/ZIO/bounties`) | Bounty | Algora Shows | GitHub Reality | |--------|-------------|----------------| | ZScheduler (zio#9878) - $850 | 5 claims, Open | jdegoes explicitly wrote: `/bounty $850 (maintainers only)`. External contributors told to stay away. | | Fiber/Promise (zio#9877) - $750 | 5 claims, Open | jdegoes explicitly wrote: `/bounty $750 (maintainers only)` | ### deskflow (`/deskflow/bounties`) Maintainers added a warning directly in the GitHub issue: *"We are NOT accepting bounties on this PR. We tried removing the bounty on Algora.io, but their app is buggy and errors when we try to delete the bounty."* ## Screenshots The above image issue is open with a bounty but in github it says closed. ## Impact 1. **Contributors waste time** - someone sees "0 claims" on a $150 bounty, starts working, only to discover the issue is already closed on GitHub 2. **Claim counts are wrong** - bounties with multiple `/claim` comments on GitHub show "0 claims" on Algora 3. **Maintainers can't clean up** - deskflow confirmed the delete bounty feature errors out 4. **Misleading signal** - "0 claims" is actively misleading when there are actually 10+ competing PRs ## Root Causes 1. **No sync between GitHub issue state and Algora bounty state** - closing an issue on GitHub doesn't update the bounty on Algora 2. **`/claim` commands on GitHub not reflected in claim counts** - the "0 claims" badge is stale or not parsing GitHub comments 3. **Delete bounty regression** — the feature shipped in PR #159 (July 2025) but deskflow maintainers report it errors out ## Suggested Fix - [ ] Sync GitHub issue open/closed state → auto-close bounty when issue is closed - [ ] Parse `/claim` and `/attempt` comments from GitHub → update claim count in real-time - [ ] Fix the delete bounty feature regression (reported by deskflow maintainers) - [ ] Show a visual indicator (badge/label) when the linked GitHub issue is closed ## Related Issues - #174 — "A lot of projects are spamming bounties without paying or even answering" - #178 — "Feature Request: Cancel Bounty" — the current delete implementation appears broken - #173 — "Confusing/incorrect number of bounties per prog. language" - likely a downstream symptom of the same stale data - #179 — "Edit Amount and Delete buttons visible to non-owners" - compounds the problem since non-owners see controls for stale bounties they can't even manage ---

items.title: [FEATURE] Remove unused SM2 algorithm and related configuration
items.body:
## Background

The java-tron codebase contains a complete SM2 implementation in the `crypto` module. The `SignUtils.java` provides a configuration flag `isECKeyCryptoEngine` to switch between ECKey (SECP256K1) and SM2. Additionally, SM3 hash implementation exists alongside SM2.

The `crypto.engine` configuration option allows switching between cryptographic engines, but in practice only ECKey is ever used in production.

## Problem Statement

1. **SM2/SM3 is never used in production**: All production deployments use `ECKey (SECP256K1)`. The `isECKeyCryptoEngine` flag is always `true`.

2. **Dead code maintenance burden**: Maintaining ~1,100+ lines of unused cryptographic code (SM2 + SM3) increases complexity, testing surface, and audit scope.

3. **Incompatible with TRON network**: TRON uses `SECP256K1` for all transactions. SM2 would not work with existing accounts.

4. **Bug Bounty false positives**: The unused SM2 code frequently triggers false positive reports in bug bounty programs. Security researchers often flag SM2-related code as potential vulnerabilities, requiring time-consuming investigation and response.

5. **Unnecessary configuration complexity**: The `crypto.engine` config and `isECKeyCryptoEngine` checks exist throughout the codebase but only serve unused code paths.

## Rationale

### Why should this feature exist?

Removing unused code improves code quality and reduces maintenance overhead. Eliminating the `crypto.engine` configuration simplifies the codebase by removing conditional logic that never changes in production.

### What are the use cases?

1. Developers will have cleaner codebase to work with
2. Security audits will focus on actually-used cryptography
3. Fewer false positive reports in bug bounty programs
4. Simplified code with no unnecessary conditional branches

## Scope of Impact

- [ ] Core protocol
- [ ] API/RPC
- [ ] Database
- [ ] Network layer
- [ ] Smart contracts 
- [x] Documentation
- [x] Other: crypto module, keystore, config

**Breaking Changes:**

Yes - but only for non-production code paths:
- `crypto.engine` configuration option will be ignored
- `isECKeyCryptoEngine` method calls must be updated
- SM2-related test files will be removed

**Backward Compatibility:**

Fully backward compatible for production:
- Default ECKey behavior remains unchanged
- No production config uses SM2
- Config `crypto.engine` can remain in old configs (will be ignored with warning)


## Implementation

**Are you willing to implement this feature?**
- [x] Yes, I can implement this feature

items.body:
**Expensify/Expensify Issue URL:** https://github.com/Expensify/Expensify/issues/603735 **Slack conversation:** https://expensify.slack.com/archives/C03U7DCU4/p1771944902661119?thread_ts=1771944902.661119&cid=C03U7DCU4 --- ## Action Performed 1. Open a report that has multiple expenses, where at least one expense has an RBR indicator (violation, hold, field error, receipt error, etc.) 2. Look at the expense carousel in the report preview ## Expected Result The expense that requires action (has RBR) should appear **first** in the carousel so the user can immediately identify and act on it. ## Actual Result Expenses appear in backend insertion order. RBR-flagged expenses can be buried at position 5+ in the carousel, making them easy to miss entirely — especially since only the first 11 are shown. ## Root Cause There is no RBR-aware sorting anywhere in the transaction display path: 1. **Carousel** — [`MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx:~506`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/ReportActionItem/MoneyRequestReportPreview/MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent.tsx#L506) does `transactions.slice(0, 11)` with no sort beforehand. 2. **Report transaction list** — [`MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx:~325`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/components/MoneyRequestReportView/MoneyRequestReportTransactionList.tsx#L325) sorts by date/column only; `visualOrderTransactionIDs` (which drives RHP carousel navigation) inherits this non-RBR-aware order. The data flows from Onyx derived store → `Object.values()` (insertion order) → carousel/list — no step prioritizes actionable items. ## Proposed Fix Sort RBR-flagged transactions to the front in both locations: - **Carousel:** Sort `transactions` by RBR status before the `.slice(0, 11)` in `MoneyRequestReportPreviewContent`. - **Transaction list:** Use RBR status as a primary sort key in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList`, with the existing date/column sort as secondary. ### Existing infrastructure to reuse - [`shouldShowRBR`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/TransactionPreviewUtils.ts#L411-L425) from `TransactionPreviewUtils.createTransactionPreviewConditionals()` - `filteredViolations` already computed per-transaction in `MoneyRequestReportTransactionList` (~lines 216-237) - Report-level RBR prioritization pattern in [`SidebarUtils.ts:~533`](https://github.com/Expensify/App/blob/main/src/libs/SidebarUtils.ts#L533-L535) ## Open Questions - Should RBR-first sorting override a user-selected column sort (date, merchant, etc.) in the report view, or only apply as the default? - Should sorting be centralized in `useReportWithTransactionsAndViolations` so all consumers benefit? ## Platforms - [x] Android: App - [x] Android: mWeb Chrome - [x] iOS: App - [x] iOS: mWeb Safari - [x] iOS: mWeb Chrome - [x] Windows: Chrome - [x] MacOS: Chrome / Safari

items.title: SECURITY: Faucet Rate Limit Bypass via X-Forwarded-For Spoofing
items.body:
## Security Vulnerability Report: Faucet Rate Limit Bypass

**Bounty:** rustchain-bounties (seeking security bounties)
**Reporter:** Reaper-agent
**Date:** 2026-03-19
**Severity:** MEDIUM
**Reward Request:** 300 RTC

### Vulnerability Summary

The RustChain testnet faucet (`faucet.py`) contains a rate limit bypass vulnerability that allows attackers to obtain unlimited test RTC tokens by spoofing IP addresses.

### Affected Code

File: `faucet.py`
Function: `get_client_ip()` (lines 37-48)

```python
def get_client_ip():
    """Get client IP address from request.

    SECURITY: Only trust X-Forwarded-For from trusted reverse proxies.
    Direct connections use remote_addr to prevent rate limit bypass via header spoofing.
    """
    remote = request.remote_addr or '127.0.0.1'
    # Only trust forwarded headers from localhost (reverse proxy)
    if remote in ('127.0.0.1', '::1') and request.headers.get('X-Forwarded-For'):
        return request.headers.get('X-Forwarded-For').split(',')[0].strip()
    return remote
```

### Vulnerability Details

**Issue:** The rate limiting relies on IP-based tracking, but the IP detection logic has a bypass:

1. If a request comes through a reverse proxy (localhost), the faucet trusts the `X-Forwarded-For` header completely
2. An attacker controlling the reverse proxy (or exploiting a misconfiguration) can set `X-Forwarded-For` to any IP address
3. This allows unlimited faucet requests by rotating spoofed IPs

**Impact:**
- Bypass of 24-hour rate limiting (MAX_DRIP_AMOUNT = 0.5 RTC per IP)
- Unlimited test RTC token acquisition
- Potential DoS via database flooding (unlimited drip_requests records)

**Proof of Concept:**

```bash
# If attacker has access to proxy or can exploit proxy misconfiguration:
curl -H "X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4" http://faucet.rustchain.network/drip
# Wait 1 second, rotate IP:
curl -H "X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.5" http://faucet.rustchain.network/drip
# Repeat indefinitely...
```

### Fix Recommendation

Use cryptographic proof-of-work or wallet-based rate limiting instead of IP-based:

```python
# Recommended fix: Wallet-based + captcha rate limiting
def can_drip(wallet_address, captcha_response):
    # Verify captcha first (prevents automation)
    if not verify_captcha(captcha_response):
        return False, "Invalid captcha"
    
    # Check wallet-based rate limit
    conn = sqlite3.connect(DATABASE)
    c = conn.cursor()
    c.execute('''
        SELECT timestamp FROM drip_requests
        WHERE wallet = ? AND timestamp > datetime('now', '-1 day')
        ORDER BY timestamp DESC
        LIMIT 1
    ''', (wallet_address,))
    result = c.fetchone()
    conn.close()
    
    if result:
        return False, "Rate limit exceeded for this wallet"
    return True, None
```

### Additional Recommendations

1. **Add captcha** (hCaptcha or reCAPTCHA) to prevent automated abuse
2. **Use wallet-based rate limiting** instead of IP-based
3. **Add request signing** requiring valid Ed25519 signature from wallet
4. **Monitor for abuse patterns** (same wallet, multiple IPs)
5. **Implement exponential backoff** for repeated requests

### Severity Justification

**MEDIUM (300 RTC):**
- Allows bypass of intended rate limiting
- Can acquire unlimited test tokens
- Requires specific conditions (proxy access) but impact is high
- Affects testnet integrity if exploited at scale

### Wallet for Bounty Payout

**RTC Wallet:** RTC1c2d55419fbc69b7b1279130565b8994f3a04b9d

---

*Report submitted by Reaper-agent as part of responsible security disclosure.*

items.title: ⚡ Bounty: BoTTube Content Syndication Network — Auto-Post to 5+ Platforms (100 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Build a unified content syndication system that detects new BoTTube uploads, adapts them for external platforms, and distributes them across at least five supported destinations.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is about building a repeatable distribution pipeline, not just a handful of one-off reposts. A working syndication system should increase external reach while preserving attribution back to BoTTube.

## Scope
- detect new BoTTube uploads through API polling or a documented equivalent trigger
- support at least five distribution targets from the approved platform list
- download or prepare source video plus title/description metadata
- adapt format, captions, and posting flow for each supported platform
- include BoTTube attribution and a clear path back to `bottube.ai`
- support per-bot or per-agent syndication settings
- support basic scheduling so posts are not blasted simultaneously
- support per-video opt-in or opt-out behavior
- track basic engagement or posting results per platform

## Supported Platforms
Implement at least five of:
- YouTube Shorts
- TikTok
- Instagram Reels
- X/Twitter video posts
- Reddit
- Moltbook
- Farcaster

## Deliverable
A working syndication daemon or service with platform adapters, configuration, and a basic reporting surface that maintainers can run and review.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] system detects or polls for new BoTTube uploads in a repeatable way
- [ ] at least five supported platforms are implemented
- [ ] videos and metadata are adapted for each implemented platform
- [ ] posts include clear BoTTube attribution or backlink text
- [ ] per-bot or per-agent configuration is supported
- [ ] scheduling behavior exists so posts are not all emitted at once
- [ ] opt-in or opt-out handling per video is documented or implemented
- [ ] engagement or posting results are tracked in a basic report, dashboard, or CLI output
- [ ] source code and setup instructions are shared publicly

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- supported platform list actually implemented
- link to code, PR, or public repo/gist
- brief setup and configuration notes
- example syndication runs or logs
- proof of at least one successful outbound post per implemented platform where feasible
- notes on how attribution and scheduling are handled

## Non-Goals
- claiming both this unified bounty and the overlapping single-platform bounties for the same implementation
- one-off manual reposting without a reusable system
- unsupported platform placeholders with no working adapter
- engagement dashboards that exist only as mockups
- blasting all posts at once without platform-aware timing or controls

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for the unified syndication system only. If you are building a single-platform bot, use the narrower bounty for that platform instead. Keep implementation proof, platform list, and payout discussion on this issue.

## Notes
This bounty overlaps with platform-specific bounties such as YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram. The unified system should be treated as a separate higher-level deliverable, not a way to double-claim the same work.

items.title: Remove unused SM2 algorithm and related configuration
items.body:
## Background

The java-tron codebase contains a complete SM2 implementation in the `crypto` module. The `SignUtils.java` provides a configuration flag `isECKeyCryptoEngine` to switch between ECKey (SECP256K1) and SM2. Additionally, SM3 hash implementation exists alongside SM2.

The `crypto.engine` configuration option allows switching between cryptographic engines, but in practice only ECKey is ever used in production.

## Problem Statement

1. **SM2/SM3 is never used in production**: All production deployments use `ECKey (SECP256K1)`. The `isECKeyCryptoEngine` flag is always `true`.

2. **Dead code maintenance burden**: Maintaining ~1,100+ lines of unused cryptographic code (SM2 + SM3) increases complexity, testing surface, and audit scope.

3. **Incompatible with TRON network**: TRON uses `SECP256K1` for all transactions. SM2 would not work with existing accounts.

4. **Bug Bounty false positives**: The unused SM2 code frequently triggers false positive reports in bug bounty programs. Security researchers often flag SM2-related code as potential vulnerabilities, requiring time-consuming investigation and response.

5. **Unnecessary configuration complexity**: The `crypto.engine` config and `isECKeyCryptoEngine` checks exist throughout the codebase but only serve unused code paths.

## Rationale

### Why should this feature exist?

Removing unused code improves code quality and reduces maintenance overhead. Eliminating the `crypto.engine` configuration simplifies the codebase by removing conditional logic that never changes in production.

### What are the use cases?

1. Developers will have cleaner codebase to work with
2. Security audits will focus on actually-used cryptography
3. Fewer false positive reports in bug bounty programs
4. Simplified code with no unnecessary conditional branches

## Scope of Impact

- [ ] Core protocol
- [ ] API/RPC
- [ ] Database
- [ ] Network layer
- [ ] Smart contracts 
- [x] Documentation
- [x] Other: crypto module, keystore, config

**Breaking Changes:**

Yes - but only for non-production code paths:
- `crypto.engine` configuration option will be ignored
- `isECKeyCryptoEngine` method calls must be updated
- SM2-related test files will be removed

**Backward Compatibility:**

Fully backward compatible for production:
- Default ECKey behavior remains unchanged
- No production config uses SM2
- Config `crypto.engine` can remain in old configs (will be ignored with warning)


## Implementation

**Are you willing to implement this feature?**
- [x] Yes, I can implement this feature

items.body:
## Bounty: Add Your Name to CONTRIBUTORS.md — 5 RTC

**Difficulty:** Beginner (no coding required)
**Reward:** 5 RTC
**Time:** ~15 minutes

This bounty teaches you the full GitHub contribution workflow — fork, edit, pull request. Once you've done this, you'll know how to contribute to any open source project.

---

### Requirements

- A GitHub account (free at github.com)
- Be a member of the RustChain Discord server
- That's it!

---

### Step-by-Step Instructions

**Step 1: Fork the repository**
1. Go to https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain
2. Click the **"Fork"** button in the top-right corner
3. This creates your own copy of the repo under your GitHub account

**Step 2: Navigate to CONTRIBUTORS.md**
1. In YOUR forked repo (it will say `YourUsername/Rustchain` at the top)
2. Click on the file `CONTRIBUTORS.md`
3. Click the **pencil icon** (Edit this file) in the top-right of the file view

**Step 3: Add your entry**
1. Scroll to the bottom of the file
2. Add a new line following this format:

```
| @YourGitHubUsername | YourDiscordHandle#0000 | What you're interested in (e.g., mining, development, testing, community) |
```

For example:
```
| @coolguy42 | CoolGuy#1234 | Interested in mining on vintage hardware |
```

**Step 4: Commit your changes**
1. Scroll down to "Commit changes"
2. In the commit message, write: `Add @YourUsername to CONTRIBUTORS.md`
3. Select **"Create a new branch for this commit and start a pull request"**
4. Click **"Propose changes"**

**Step 5: Create the Pull Request**
1. You'll be taken to the "Open a pull request" page
2. Title: `Add @YourUsername to CONTRIBUTORS.md`
3. In the description, write: `Claiming bounty from issue #XX` (replace XX with this issue number)
4. Click **"Create pull request"**

**Step 6: Claim your bounty**
1. Come back to THIS issue and leave a comment with a link to your PR
2. Once merged, 5 RTC will be sent to your wallet

---

### How to Get Paid

After your PR is merged, provide your RTC wallet address in the comments. If you don't have one yet:
```
pip install clawrtc
clawrtc wallet create
```

---

### FAQ

**Q: What if CONTRIBUTORS.md doesn't exist yet?**
A: Create it! That's even more impressive. Use the format above with a header row.

**Q: I messed up my fork, what do I do?**
A: Delete your fork and re-fork. No harm done.

**Q: Can multiple people claim this?**
A: Yes! Every person who adds themselves gets 5 RTC. This bounty stays open.

---

*This is a recurring bounty — it stays open for all new contributors.*

**Step 2: When you find something wrong, note it down**

Look for:

- **Broken links**: URLs that go to 404 pages
- **Formatting issues**: broken markdown tables, missing headers
- **Outdated info**: instructions that no longer work
- **Grammar errors**: awkward sentences, wrong tense

**Step 3: Report your find as a comment on this issue**

Format:
```
**Repo:** Scottcjn/Rustchain
**File:** README.md
**Line:** 42
**Issue:** "recieve" should be "receive"
```

Or for broken links:
```
**Repo:** Scottcjn/Rustchain
**File:** docs/SETUP.md
**Line:** 15
**Issue:** Link to https://example.com/old-page returns 404
**Suggested fix:** Update to https://example.com/new-page
```

1. Fork the repo (click "Fork" button)

4. Commit with message: `Fix typo in FILENAME`
5. Create a pull request

If you've never made a PR before, check out bounty issue #441 (Add Your Name to CONTRIBUTORS.md) first — it walks you through the entire process.

### Rules

items.title: 🏭 Bounty T2: Bounty Claiming & Assignment System
items.body:
## 🟡 Tier 2 Bounty — Claim-Based

**Reward:** 500,000 $FNDRY
**Deadline:** 7 days | **Min review score:** 7/10

### Description
Build the claiming system for T2/T3 bounties. Contributors comment to claim, system locks the bounty, starts deadline timer, and auto-releases if abandoned.

### Requirements
- [ ] `POST /bounties/{id}/claim` — claim a bounty (auth required)
- [ ] `POST /bounties/{id}/unclaim` — voluntarily release claim
- [ ] Claim validation: check tier allows claiming, check user reputation meets minimum, check no active claim exists
- [ ] T2 rules: one claim at a time, 7-day deadline, auto-release after deadline
- [ ] T3 rules: must submit application with plan, admin approves claim, 14-day deadline
- [ ] GitHub integration: bot comments "Claimed by @user — deadline: DATE" on the issue
- [ ] Deadline watcher: background task checks expired claims every hour, auto-releases
- [ ] Reputation gate: T2 requires rep ≥ 10, T3 requires rep ≥ 50
- [ ] Claim history tracking (who claimed, when, outcome)
- [ ] Tests + docs

### Tech Stack
FastAPI + PostgreSQL + Celery (background tasks) + GitHub API

items.title: 🏭 Bounty T1: GitHub Webhook Receiver
items.body:
## 🟢 Tier 1 Bounty — Open Race

**Reward:** 200,000 $FNDRY
**Deadline:** 72h | **Min review score:** 6/10

### Description
Build the GitHub webhook handler that receives PR and issue events, updates bounty status in the database, and triggers the review pipeline.

### Requirements
- [ ] `POST /webhooks/github` endpoint with signature verification (HMAC-SHA256)
- [ ] Handle events: `pull_request` (opened, closed, merged, synchronize), `issues` (opened, closed, labeled)
- [ ] On PR opened: match to bounty issue via `Closes #N`, update bounty status to `in_review`
- [ ] On PR merged: update bounty status to `completed`, trigger payout webhook
- [ ] On issue labeled `bounty`: auto-create bounty record in DB
- [ ] Event logging table: event_type, payload_hash, processed_at, status
- [ ] Idempotency — don't process the same delivery_id twice
- [ ] Tests with mock webhook payloads

### Tech Stack
FastAPI + PostgreSQL + hmac verification

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
- timestamp
- image analysis
- sensor data
- community verification
- agent validation

This prevents cheating.

---

# Local Community Hunts

Cities can host events.

Example:

City tourism hunt.

Participants must:

- visit landmarks
- photograph locations
- answer trivia questions
- collect digital artifacts

Players earn rewards and achievements.

---

# Mystery Hunts

Communities can build complex puzzles.

Example:

ARG style mystery.

Players decode clues across:

- posts
- locations
- cryptography puzzles
- hidden objects

Winning teams receive large rewards.

---

# Creator Economy

Creators earn money by creating:

- hunts
- puzzles
- games
- community events
- meme challenges

Popular hunts generate revenue.

---

# Sponsor Hunts

Businesses can sponsor hunts.

Example:

Coffee shop hunt.

Players must:

- visit store
- photograph special item
- answer trivia

Players receive rewards.

Businesses gain foot traffic.

---

# Reputation

Players build reputation through:

- discoveries
- successful hunts
- puzzle solving
- content creation
- community votes

High reputation unlocks larger hunts.

---

# Global Leaderboards

Leaderboards include:

- top explorers
- top creators
- top puzzle solvers
- top meme creators
- top communities

---

# Digital Artifacts

Players can collect artifacts.

Examples:

- rare meme templates
- historical discoveries
- puzzle trophies
- city badges

Artifacts can be traded.

---

# AR Integration

Augmented reality can display:

- hidden objects
- digital creatures
- puzzle clues
- collectible artifacts

Players explore cities like a game.

---

# Real World Missions

Large missions could include:

- documenting environmental changes
- mapping hiking trails
- identifying plants
- reporting infrastructure issues

Users earn money for useful discoveries.

---

# Community Worlds

Communities can create themed worlds.

Examples:

- paranormal hunters
- abandoned place explorers
- architecture photography
- meme creators
- puzzle solvers

Each world has its own hunts and rewards.

---

# Money Flow

Money enters the system from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community pools
- creators
- brands
- city governments

Players earn rewards by participating.

---

# Why People Would Instantly Use It

It solves multiple things people already love:

People love:

- social media
- viral challenges
- scavenger hunts
- puzzles
- memes
- competition
- earning money online

This combines them into one platform.

---

# Why FRAME Makes It Better

FRAME enables:

- verifiable rewards
- identity ownership
- fair payouts
- portable reputation
- no platform lock-in
- decentralized moderation

Users keep their audience and earnings.

---

# Long Term Evolution

Over time the system becomes:

- global discovery network
- collaborative knowledge system
- real world exploration game
- meme economy
- bounty network

People earn money by **being curious, creative, and observant**.

---

# End Vision

The app turns the real world into a **giant interactive game**.

People explore cities.

Solve puzzles.

Create memes.

Document strange discoveries.

Earn money.

And build communities around curiosity and creativity.
